text: Five trocars are placed for the robotic Whipple procedure, including a 12mm trocar at the umbilicus and another at the right midclavicular line, with three additional 8mm robotic cannulas. The abdomen is inspected for metastatic disease, and the round ligament is taken down as a vascularized pedicle. The gallbladder is sutured to the anterior abdominal wall with a V-lock suture, providing exposure of the portohepatous. The gastrocolic ligament is divided using a vessel sealant device, and the stomach is mobilized along its greater curvature. Mobilization continues posteriorly along the distal antrum and pylorus, and the lesser curvature is also mobilized. The duodenum is transected using a robotic stapler just distal to the pylorus. Intraoperative ultrasound is used to identify key vascular structures and the tumor's proximity to them.

The surgeon carefully dissects and suture ligates the right gastric artery, followed by the gastroduodenal artery, which is controlled with hemlock clips and divided. The inferior border and neck of the pancreas are dissected and mobilized, with umbilical tape placed posteriorly for traction. The neck of the pancreas is transected using monopolar scissors with saline irrigation to minimize tissue charring. A window is created in the transverse mesocolon to identify and transect the jejunum 15 cm distal from the ligament of Treitz using a robotic stapler. The uncinate process is dissected away from the superior mesenteric vessels, and branches from the superior mesenteric vein are suture ligated. The cystic duct and artery are clipped and divided, and the common hepatic duct is transected above the cystic duct takeoff. The specimen is placed in a retrieval bag and removed through the umbilical trocar site for pathology.

The surgeon begins the reconstruction phase with a two-layer end-to-side pancreaticojejunostomy. The posterior layer is sutured using a 5-0 monofilament in a running fashion, while a small enterotomy is made in the jejunum for the anastomosis, secured with interrupted 6-0 monofilament sutures over a small pancreatic duct stent. The anterior layer is completed with a running 5-0 monofilament suture, and the anastomosis is wrapped with a round ligament pedicle flap. A hepatico-jejunostomy is created 10 to 15 centimeters downstream from the pancreaticojejunostomy using 5-0 monofilament sutures. The gallbladder is dissected free from the gallbladder fossa and removed from the anterior abdominal wall. An anticholic duodenal jejunostomy is performed approximately 50 centimeters from the biliary anastomosis using absorbable monofilament sutures in a running fashion.
The surgeon places a suction drain in the right upper quadrant, adjacent to the bile duct and pancreatic anastomosis, after removing the remaining specimens.
